audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a man called Jack giving advice to a friend about surfing holidays.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Jack, I'm thinking of taking the kids to the seaside on a surfing holiday this summer, and I wanted to ask your advice, as I know you're such an expert.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I don't know about that.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: But yes, I've done a bit of surfing over the years.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I'd thoroughly recommend it.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think it's the kind of holiday all the family can enjoy together.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: The thing about surfing is that it's great for all ages and all abilities.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: My youngest started when he was only 3.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Wow!
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But it's quite physically demanding, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've heard you need to be pretty fit.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, you'll certainly learn more quickly, and won't tire as easily.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Well, that should be OK for us.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: You've been surfing a few times in Ireland, haven't you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, there's some great surfing there, which people don't always realize.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And which locations would you recommend?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There seem to be quite a few.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, there are loads.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Last year we went to County Donegal.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: There are several great places to surf there.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: What about in County Clare?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I read that's also really good for surfing.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it is.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I've been there a few times.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Most people go to Lahinch.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: My kids love it there, the waves aren't too challenging, and the town is very lively.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Are there good hotels there?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, some very nice ones.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And there are also a few basic hostels and camp sites.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: It's great if you need lessons, as the surf schools are excellent.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, and there's lots to see in the area.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Like those well-known cliffs, I've forgotten the name of them.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Oh, don't worry, I can look them up.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've also been surfing in County Mayo.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Which is less well known for surfing, but we had a really good time.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That was a few years ago, when the kids were younger.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: There's a good surf school at Carrowniskey beach.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: How do you spell that?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: CARROWNISKEY.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I put the kids into the surf camp they run during the summer for 10 to 16 year olds.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How long was that for?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: 3 hours every day for a week.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: It was perfect.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: They were so tired out after that.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I can imagine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: One thing we did while the kids were surfing was to rent some kayaks to have a look around the bay, which is nearby.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: It's really beautiful.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Oh, I'd love to do that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, the only time I went to Ireland, it rained practically every day.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Yes, that can be a problem, but you can surf in the rain, you know.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: It doesn't have the same appeal somehow.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, the weather's been fine the last couple of years when I've been there, but actually it tends to rain more in August than in the spring or autumn.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: September's my favorite month, because the water is warmer then.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: The only problem is that the kids are back to school then.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I know, but one good thing about Irish summers is that it doesn't get too hot.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: The average temperature is about 19 degrees, and it usually doesn't go above 25 degrees.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That sounds all right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now what about costs?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Surfing is a pretty cheap holiday really, the only cost is the hire of equipment.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: You can expect to pay a daily rate of about €30 for the hire of a wetsuit and board, but you can save about €40 if you hire by the week.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That's not too bad.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, it's important to make sure you get good quality wetsuits.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: You'll all get too cold if you don't, and make sure you also get boots.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: They keep your feet warm, and it's easier to surf with them on too.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, well, thanks very much for that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear the manager of a childcare service at a primary school talking to parents at an open day about the school's childcare service offered before and after school.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: My name's Mrs Carter, and I run the before and after school extended hours childcare service.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: I hope you've had a chance to have a good look around the school, and talk to staff and pupils.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: I know that many of you are interested in using our childcare service when your child joins the school, and perhaps you already know something about it, but for those that don't, I'll go through the main details now.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We offer child care for children from the ages of 4 to 11, both before and after school.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: I know that many parents who work find this service invaluable.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: You can leave your child with us, safe in the knowledge that they will be extremely well cared for.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We are insured to provide care for up to 70 children, although we rarely have this many attending at any one session.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: I think we generally expect around 50 to 60 children for the afternoon sessions.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: And about half that number for the breakfast sessions, although we currently do have 70 children registered with us, not all of these attend every day.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: It's 10 years since we began offering an extended hours service, and we've come a long way during that time.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: When we first opened, we only had about 20 children attending regularly.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We try to keep our costs as low as we can, and we think we provide very good value for money. For the afternoon sessions, which run from 3.30 until 6 p.m., it's £7.20.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: But if you prefer, you can pay for one hour only, which costs £3.50, or two hours which costs £5.70.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: The cost of the child care includes food and snacks.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: They'll be given breakfast in the morning, and in the afternoon, a healthy snack as soon as they finish school.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: At 5 pm, children are given something more substantial, such as pasta or a casserole.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Please inform us of any allergies that your child might have.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: And we'll make sure they're offered a suitable alternative.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: As you may know, the childcare service runs through the school holidays from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We offer a really varied and exciting program to keep the children entertained.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We don't want them to feel as if they are still at school.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: It will also feel different because they'll get the chance to make new friends with children from other schools.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Spaces are available for them, because a lot of our term time children don't always attend during the holiday.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: In the past, parents have asked if children over the age of 11 are allowed to come with their younger brothers and sisters, but I'm afraid we're unable to do this.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Because of the type of insurance we have.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So now let me tell you about some of the activities that your child can do during the after-school sessions, as well as being able to use the playground equipment, computers, and the library.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: There is usually at least one special activity that children can do each day.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: For example, Spanish.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We have a specialist teacher coming in every Thursday.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: To give a basic introduction to the language through games and songs.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: She does two sessions: one for the over 8s, and one for the younger children.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: This is the only activity which we have to make an extra charge for, but it's well worth it.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Once a week the children have the opportunity to do some music.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We're very lucky that one of our staff is a member of a folk band.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: On Mondays, she teaches singing and percussion to groups of children.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We do rely on parental support for this, so if any of you sing or play an instrument, and would be prepared to help out at these sessions, we'd be delighted.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Painting continues to be one of the most popular activities.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: To begin with, we weren't keen on offering this because of the extra mess involved, but children kept asking if they could do some art.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: And so we finally gave in.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Art is great for helping the children to relax after working hard at school all day.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Yoga is something that we've been meaning to introduce for some time, but haven't been able to find anyone available to teach it until now, that is.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: So we'll see how this goes.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Hopefully, children will benefit in all sorts of ways from this.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: Cooking is another popular activity.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: They make a different sort of cake or pizza or bread each week, although the younger children love doing it.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: We found that the mess was just too much, so we've decided to restrict this to the over 8s, as they are better able to clean up after themselves.
audioscript.speaker: MRS CARTER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: There is usually at least one special activity that children can do each day.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: For example, Spanish.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: We have a specialist teacher coming in every Thursday.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: To give a basic introduction to the language through games and songs.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: She does two sessions: one for the over 8s, and one for the younger children.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: This is the only activity which we have to make an extra charge for, but it's well worth it.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: Once a week the children have the opportunity to do some music.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: We're very lucky that one of our staff is a member of a folk band.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: On Mondays, she teaches singing and percussion to groups of children.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: We do rely on parental support for this, so if any of you sing or play an instrument, and would be prepared to help out at these sessions, we'd be delighted.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: Painting continues to be one of the most popular activities.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: To begin with, we weren't keen on offering this because of the extra mess involved, but children kept asking if they could do some art.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: And so we finally gave in.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: Art is great for helping the children to relax after working hard at school all day.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: Yoga is something that we've been meaning to introduce for some time, but haven't been able to find anyone available to teach it until now, that is.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: So we'll see how this goes.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: Hopefully, children will benefit in all sorts of ways from this.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: Cooking is another popular activity.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: They make a different sort of cake or pizza or bread each week, although the younger children love doing it.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: We found that the mess was just too much, so we've decided to restrict this to the over 8s, as they are better able to clean up after themselves.
utterances.speaker: MRS CARTER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO facts are given about the school’s extended hours childcare service?
options: It started recently.
More children attend after school than before school.
An average of 50 children attend in the mornings.
A child cannot attend both the before and after school sessions.
The maximum number of children who can attend is 70.

items.title: How much does childcare cost for a complete afternoon session per child?
What does the manager say about food?
options: Children with allergies should bring their own food.
Children may bring healthy snacks with them.
Children are given a proper meal at 5 p.m.

items.title: What is different about arrangements in the school holidays?
options: Children from other schools can attend.
Older children can attend.
A greater number of children can attend.

text: What information is given about each of the following activities on offer?
textReadable: What information is given about each of the following activities on offer?

items: Spanish  <input>
Music    <input>
Painting <input>
Yoga     <input>
Cooking  <input>
body.title: Activities

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear Holly, a student on an events management course, talking to her tutor about her work placement.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Doctor Green.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I'm here to talk to you about my work placement.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, it's Holly, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: So, which work placement have you chosen?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I decided to go for the Orion Stadium placement.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: The event I'll be managing is one where I'm helping to set up a sports competition for primary school children.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Yep, that's always a popular placement, even though it can be tougher than you think working with children.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I know, but it's the fresh air that attracts me.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Organizing something indoors doesn't have the same appeal, even though it might be fun.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: OK, so obviously safety is going to be one of your key concerns for this event.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I've already thought about that.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I'll need to make sure none of the equipment's damaged.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Uh. Well you'll be working with schools, so the equipment will be their responsibility.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: However, the grounds and what goes on there will be yours.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, I see.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: That'll include keeping everyone within the boundary once they're in their kit and on the field.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You'll need to inspect areas like changing rooms as well, for anything someone can trip over.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: But your main priority will be not to lose anyone.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh. Right.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I'll need staff to help with that.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: And don't forget about the spectators.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Hmm. I was thinking that many of them will be parents who could help run the event.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I wouldn't rely on that.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: They'll be more interested in filming their children than volunteering.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I'll need to make sure they don't interfere with events doing that.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: And that's not always easy, especially when a proud parent's trying to get a snap of their child,
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: and you want them to move elsewhere.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, uh, what about the scheduling?
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: With sporting events, there are all sorts of things that can alter the timetable.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Like rain for instance, though so far we've always been lucky with that.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, and I was thinking about what to do if someone got hurt as well.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I know that last year that caused a terrible delay.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: You have to be prepared for such things.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, what if a match ends in a draw?
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Do you let the teams keep going until someone wins?
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: That'll be up to you, and again, you need to plan for it.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, the aim of your work placement is to give you the opportunity to develop the skills that an events manager needs.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So, let's talk about those a bit.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, I think my communication skills are pretty good.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I can talk on the phone to people, and book venues, and that kind of thing.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Just remember, it isn't only about what you say.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: If you meet someone face to face and want to persuade them to be a sponsor, for example.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, I'll dress up for that, sure.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Good. Let's go on to think about your organizational skills.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You're working in a very people-based industry, and that means things won't always go to plan.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I guess it's being prepared to make changes that matters.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You may have to make an on-the-spot change to a timetable because of a problem you hadn't anticipated.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Just do it! OK.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: How's your time management these days?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I'm working on it.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I'm certainly better when I have a deadline, which is why this work suits me.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Yes, but it's how you respond as that deadline approaches.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I know I've got to look calm, even if I'm in a panic.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Just think to yourself, no one must know I'm under pressure.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, even though I'm multitasking like crazy.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Another skill that events managers need is creativity.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Often your client has what we call the big picture idea, but it's up to the events manager to think of all the fine points that go to making it work.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Right, so I need to listen carefully to that idea, and then fill in all the gaps.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: That's right, and you'll have a team working under you.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So another key skill is leadership.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Your team may have lots of ideas too, but you've got to make the ultimate choices.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Do we have refreshments inside or out for example?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Isn't it better to be democratic?
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: It's a nice idea, but you have the ultimate responsibility.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So believe in what you think best.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Be prepared to say yes, that's a good idea, but it won't work here.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I see what you mean.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: What about the networking side of things?
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: I know it's an area that a lot of students worry about, because we don't have much experience to offer others.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: But even without it, you can still be an interesting person with useful ideas.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And the more people you impress, the better.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I guess that will help me when I apply for a real job.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Think ahead, remember what your ambitions are.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And keep them in mind.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh. Definitely.
audioscript.speaker: HOLLY

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Holly has chosen the Orion Stadium placement because
options: it involves children.
it is outdoors.
it sounds like fun.

items.title: Which aspect of safety does Dr Green emphasise most?
options: ensuring children stay in the stadium
checking the equipment children will use
removing obstacles in changing rooms

items.title: What does Dr Green say about the spectators?
options: They can be hard to manage.
They make useful volunteers.
They shouldn't take photographs.

items.title: What has affected the schedule in the past?
options: bad weather
an injury
extra time

text: What do Holly and her tutor agree is an important aspect of each of the following events management skills?
textReadable: What do Holly and her tutor agree is an important aspect of each of the following events management skills?

list: doing one thing at a time
items: Communication   <input>
Organisation    <input>
Time management <input>
Creativity      <input>
Leadership      <input>
Networking      <input>
body.title: Events management skills

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a zoology student giving a presentation on bird migration.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Scientists believe that a majority of the Earth's bird population migrate in some fashion or other.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some travel seasonally for relatively short distances.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Such as birds that move from their winter habitats in lowlands to mountaintops for the summers. Others, like the Arctic Tern, travel more than 25,000 miles seasonally.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Between the northern and southern poles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Bird migration has been studied over many centuries through a variety of observations, but until relatively recently, where birds went to in the winter was considered something of a mystery.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The lack of modern science and technology led to many theories that we now recognize as error filled, and even somewhat amusing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Take hibernation theory for example, 2000 years ago, it was commonly believed that when birds left an area, they went underwater to hibernate in the seas and oceans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another theory for the regular appearance and disappearance of birds was that they spent winter hidden in mud, till the weather changed and food became abundant again.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The theory that some birds hibernate persisted until experiments were done on caged birds in the 1940s which demonstrated that birds have no hibernation instinct.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the earliest naturalists and philosophers from ancient Greece was Aristotle, who was the first writer to discuss the disappearance and reappearance of some bird species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At certain times of year, he developed the theory of transmutation, the seasonal change of one species into another, by observing redstarts and robins.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He observed that in the autumn, small birds called redstarts began to lose their feathers, which convinced Aristotle that they changed into robins for the winter.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And back into redstarts in the summer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These assumptions are understandable, given that this pair of species are similar in shape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But are a classic example of an incorrect interpretation based on correct observations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The most bizarre theory was put forward by an English amateur scientist, Charles Morton, in the 17th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He wrote a surprisingly well regarded paper.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Claiming that birds migrate to the moon and back every year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He came to this conclusion as the only logical explanation for the total disappearance of some species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the key moments in the development of migration theory came in 1822 when a white stork was shot in Germany.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This particular stork made history because of the long spear in its neck, which incredibly had not killed it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Everyone immediately realized this spear was definitely not European.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It turned out to be a spear from a tribe in central Africa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was a truly defining moment in the history of ornithology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because it was the first evidence that storks spend their winters in sub-Saharan Africa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can still see the arrow stork in the zoological collection of the university of Rostock in Germany.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People gradually became aware that European birds moved south in autumn and north in summer, but didn't know much about it until the practice of catching birds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And putting rings on their legs became established.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before this, very little information was available about the actual destinations of particular species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And how they traveled there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People speculated that larger birds provided a kind of taxi service for smaller birds by carrying them on their backs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This idea came about, because it seemed impossible that small birds weighing only a few grams could fly over vast oceans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This idea was supported by observations of bird behavior, such as the harassment of larger birds by smaller birds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The development of bird ringing, by a Danish school teacher, Hans Christian Cornelius Mortensen, made many discoveries possible.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is still common practice today, and relies upon what is known as recovery.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is when ringed birds are found dead in the place they have migrated to.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And identified.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Huge amounts of data were gathered in the early part of the 20th century, and for the first time in history, people understood where birds actually went to in winter.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1931, an atlas was published showing where the most common species of European birds migrated to.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: More recent theories about bird migration...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear a zoology student giving a presentation on bird migration.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Scientists believe that a majority of the Earth's bird population migrate in some fashion or other.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some travel seasonally for relatively short distances.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Such as birds that move from their winter habitats in lowlands to mountaintops for the summers. Others, like the Arctic Tern, travel more than 25,000 miles seasonally.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Between the northern and southern poles.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Bird migration has been studied over many centuries through a variety of observations, but until relatively recently, where birds went to in the winter was considered something of a mystery.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The lack of modern science and technology led to many theories that we now recognize as error filled, and even somewhat amusing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Take hibernation theory for example, 2000 years ago, it was commonly believed that when birds left an area, they went underwater to hibernate in the seas and oceans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another theory for the regular appearance and disappearance of birds was that they spent winter hidden in mud, till the weather changed and food became abundant again.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The theory that some birds hibernate persisted until experiments were done on caged birds in the 1940s which demonstrated that birds have no hibernation instinct.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the earliest naturalists and philosophers from ancient Greece was Aristotle, who was the first writer to discuss the disappearance and reappearance of some bird species.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At certain times of year, he developed the theory of transmutation, the seasonal change of one species into another, by observing redstarts and robins.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He observed that in the autumn, small birds called redstarts began to lose their feathers, which convinced Aristotle that they changed into robins for the winter.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And back into redstarts in the summer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These assumptions are understandable, given that this pair of species are similar in shape.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But are a classic example of an incorrect interpretation based on correct observations.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The most bizarre theory was put forward by an English amateur scientist, Charles Morton, in the 17th century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He wrote a surprisingly well regarded paper.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Claiming that birds migrate to the moon and back every year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He came to this conclusion as the only logical explanation for the total disappearance of some species.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the key moments in the development of migration theory came in 1822 when a white stork was shot in Germany.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This particular stork made history because of the long spear in its neck, which incredibly had not killed it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Everyone immediately realized this spear was definitely not European.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It turned out to be a spear from a tribe in central Africa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was a truly defining moment in the history of ornithology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because it was the first evidence that storks spend their winters in sub-Saharan Africa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can still see the arrow stork in the zoological collection of the university of Rostock in Germany.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: People gradually became aware that European birds moved south in autumn and north in summer, but didn't know much about it until the practice of catching birds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And putting rings on their legs became established.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before this, very little information was available about the actual destinations of particular species.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And how they traveled there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: People speculated that larger birds provided a kind of taxi service for smaller birds by carrying them on their backs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This idea came about, because it seemed impossible that small birds weighing only a few grams could fly over vast oceans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This idea was supported by observations of bird behavior, such as the harassment of larger birds by smaller birds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The development of bird ringing, by a Danish school teacher, Hans Christian Cornelius Mortensen, made many discoveries possible.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is still common practice today, and relies upon what is known as recovery.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is when ringed birds are found dead in the place they have migrated to.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And identified.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Huge amounts of data were gathered in the early part of the 20th century, and for the first time in history, people understood where birds actually went to in winter.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1931, an atlas was published showing where the most common species of European birds migrated to.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: More recent theories about bird migration...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Bird Migration Theory
